sanskrit_devanagari:
आत्मौपम्येन सर्वत्र समं पश्यति योऽर्जुन ।
सुखं वा यदि वा दुःखं स योगी परमो मतः ॥ ३२ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
ātmaupamyena sarvatra samaṁ paśyati yo 'rjuna |
sukhaṁ vā yadi vā duḥkhaṁ sa yogī paramo mataḥ || 32 ||

anvaya.meaning: by analogy with one's own self

translations.verbatim_quote: He is a perfect yogī who, by comparison to his own self, sees the true equality of all beings, in both their happiness and their distress, O Arjuna!
I regard them to be perfect yogis who see the true equality of all living beings and respond to the joys and sorrows of others as if they were their own.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1567 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: One who is Kṛṣṇa conscious is a perfect yogī; he is aware of everyone’s happiness and distress by dint of his own personal experience. The cause of the distress of a living entity is forgetfulness of his relationship with God.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.32 Atma-aupamyena: Atma means the self, i.e. oneself. That by which a comparison is made is an upama. The abstract from of that is aupamya. Atma-aupamya means a standard as would be applicable to oneself. O Arjuna, yah, he who; pasyati, judges; sarvatra, in all beings; samam, by the same
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-32-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.32 (iv) He who — because of the similarity between his own self and other selves, as they are all constituted similarly of uncontracted knowledge in their essential being — views the pleasures in the form of the birth of a son and the sorrows in the form of the death of a son of his own
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-32-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)

disagreements_among_translators.word: ātmaupamyena (by analogy with one's own self)

positions.rendering: by comparison with his own self / sees the true equality of all
what is pleasurable to me is pleasurable to all; what is painful to me is painful to all — therefore harms no one
the yogin sees in all his own state — the pleasure-and-pain that is mine is theirs

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three traditions read ātmaupamya as the practice of mapping one's own pleasure and pain onto others. Sankara emphasises the ethical corollary (ahiṃsā); Ramanuja emphasises structural identification; Prab. flattens to 'true equality.' The operational claim — empathy-by-self-comparison as the diagnostic of the supreme yogi — is preserved.

literal_meaning: By analogy with one's own self, the one who sees everywhere alike — whether pleasure or pain — that yogi, O Arjuna, is regarded as supreme.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.32 names the diagnostic of the supreme yogi: ātmaupamyena, by analogy-with-the-self. The yogi reads other beings' pleasure and pain by mapping them onto his own — and sees them samam, the same. This is not affective merging; it is structural identification grounded in the realisation of 6.29-6.31. Sankara's reading: the supreme yogi sees everywhere that what is pleasurable to himself is pleasurable to all beings, and what is painful to himself is painful to them; thus he does no harm to anyone. Ramanuja's reading: by ātmaupamya, the yogin sees in all his own state of pleasure or pain, which confers on him the title parama. Both traditions cohere on the operational claim: empathy-by-self-comparison is the diagnostic of the highest yogi. This verse pre-figures the dialectical turn at 6.33.
